_notes: Training examples for the Documenter agent. Based on Clery Act reporting standards, ATIXA guidelines, and Virginia Tech CRCPE policy. Severity reflects real-world campus incident classification used by university bias response teams.

high: Physical assault, unwanted physical contact, or blocking movement
Direct verbal or written threats — 'leave campus', 'you'll regret this', 'I know where you live'
Repeated or ongoing harassment (second or third incident mentioned)
Stalking behavior — following, monitoring schedule, repeated unwanted contact
Property theft or vandalism — stealing belongings, damaging property, hiding disability equipment
Public targeting with personal information — photo used, name posted, identity exposed
Hate symbols or slurs directed at a person's residence (dorm door, car, locker)
Any incident creating immediate safety concern or fear for physical safety
Interference with disability accommodations (wheelchair, hearing aids, etc.)
medium: Verbal harassment or slurs directed at the student in person
Discriminatory statements from authority figures (professors, TAs, advisors, RAs)
Exclusion from academic activities, study groups, or lab partnerships based on identity
Hostile environment in academic or professional setting affecting participation
Dismissal or retaliation when concerns are raised
Pattern of microaggressions from the same person or group
Online harassment not involving threats or identity theft
Discriminatory grading, assignment, or academic treatment
Bias incident witnessed by others who did not intervene (especially authority figures)
low: Single overheard comment not directed at the student
Appears unintentional or ignorant rather than malicious
Casual insensitive remark with no pattern of behavior
No authority figure involved, no academic impact
Student is uncertain whether bias was the motivation
Minimal or no impact on safety, academics, or daily life

critical_rules: Property theft or damage is ALWAYS at least medium severity — it is a Clery Act reportable crime
If the theft or damage appears bias-motivated (slur left behind, targeting based on identity), it is HIGH
If the theft has no clear bias motivation stated, classify as medium and bias_category as 'other'
Never classify theft, vandalism, or property damage as LOW
Repeated incidents are always one level higher than a single incident would be
Threats — even vague ones — are always HIGH
Physical contact of any kind is always HIGH

incident_type_guidelines.verbal: Spoken comments, jokes, slurs, or statements made in person or over audio/video call
incident_type_guidelines.physical: Any unwanted physical contact, assault, blocking movement, shoving, or physical intimidation
incident_type_guidelines.written: Notes, letters, flyers, posters, graffiti, or other physical written materials
incident_type_guidelines.online: Social media posts, messaging apps, email, forums, group chats, or any digital communication
incident_type_guidelines.property: Vandalism, theft, damage to belongings, hiding or tampering with personal property
incident_type_guidelines.other: Incidents that do not fit the above categories or clearly involve multiple types

bias_category_guidelines.race: Based on racial identity, skin color, or racial stereotypes
bias_category_guidelines.ethnicity: Based on ethnic background, cultural identity, or ethnic stereotypes
bias_category_guidelines.religion: Based on religious beliefs, practices, symbols, or religious identity
bias_category_guidelines.gender: Based on gender identity, gender expression, or gender stereotypes
bias_category_guidelines.sexual_orientation: Based on sexual orientation or perceived sexual orientation
bias_category_guidelines.disability: Based on physical or mental disability, accommodation needs, or disability status
bias_category_guidelines.national_origin: Based on country of origin, citizenship status, accent, or nationality
bias_category_guidelines.other: Incident does not clearly fit a protected category, or involves general theft/property crime without stated bias motivation

examples.raw_text: Someone stole my laptop from the library.
examples.reasoning: Theft of personal property is a Clery Act reportable crime — always at least medium. No bias motivation stated, so bias_category is other.

examples.raw_text: Someone stole my laptop and left a note with a racial slur on my desk.
examples.reasoning: Bias-motivated theft with written slur — property crime combined with targeted racial harassment.

examples.raw_text: My backpack was stolen from my dorm room. I think someone broke in.
examples.reasoning: Theft from personal residence (dorm) with possible break-in creates immediate safety concern — high severity.

examples.raw_text: Someone in my class made racist jokes about my ethnicity during a group discussion. The professor heard it but didn't say anything.
examples.reasoning: Verbal harassment in academic setting with authority figure present but not intervening. No direct threat or physical contact.

examples.raw_text: A student pushed me and called me a slur related to my sexual orientation in the parking lot after class.
examples.reasoning: Physical contact combined with verbal slur — constitutes assault.

examples.raw_text: I found graffiti with anti-Semitic symbols on my dorm room door. This is the second time this month.
examples.reasoning: Repeated targeting of personal residence with hate symbols — creates unsafe environment and is a pattern.

examples.raw_text: Someone created a fake social media account using my photo and posted Islamophobic content pretending to be me.
examples.reasoning: Identity theft combined with religious harassment — potential reputational damage and public targeting.

examples.raw_text: A TA made a comment suggesting students with disabilities shouldn't be in advanced courses. I have a documented disability.
examples.reasoning: Discriminatory statement from authority figure affecting academic environment, but no direct threat.

examples.raw_text: I overheard classmates making jokes about my accent and imitating the way I speak. They didn't know I could hear them.
examples.reasoning: Overheard rather than directed at student, appears unintentional, single instance, no authority figure.

examples.raw_text: Someone spray-painted a homophobic slur on my car in the campus parking lot. I have photos.
examples.reasoning: Vandalism of personal property with hate speech — criminal damage, bias-motivated.

examples.raw_text: A group chat for my major has been sharing memes that mock women in STEM. When I said it made me uncomfortable, they told me to leave if I can't take a joke.
examples.reasoning: Hostile academic environment, dismissal of concerns when raised — affects participation in major.

examples.raw_text: During a presentation, another student implied people from my country are terrorists. The whole class heard it.
examples.reasoning: Public accusation of terrorism based on national origin in academic setting — creates hostile environment.

examples.raw_text: I received an anonymous note under my door with a racial slur and a threat saying I should leave campus.
examples.reasoning: Written threat combined with racial slur, direct intimidation at residence.

examples.raw_text: My lab partner refuses to work with me and told others it's because of my gender identity. Now no one wants to partner with me.
examples.reasoning: Exclusion from academic activities based on gender identity — affects coursework and academic access.

examples.raw_text: Someone keeps moving my wheelchair when I leave it outside the classroom. Yesterday they hid it in a different building.
examples.reasoning: Repeated interference with disability accommodation equipment — creates safety risk and is ongoing.

examples.raw_text: A student made an offhand comment about my religion during a casual conversation. It seemed ignorant but not intentionally mean.
examples.reasoning: Single instance, appears unintentional, no pattern, no academic impact.

examples.raw_text: I've been getting threatening DMs on Instagram from someone who knows my class schedule. They've made comments about my race and said they're watching me.
examples.reasoning: Stalking behavior combined with racial harassment and direct threats — immediate safety concern.

examples.raw_text: During a debate in class, a student said women are naturally worse at logic. The professor didn't correct them.
examples.reasoning: Biased statement in academic debate context, not targeted at a specific individual.

examples.raw_text: Someone posted flyers around campus with hateful rhetoric about immigrants. My photo from the student directory was included.
examples.reasoning: Public targeting with personal photo, widespread distribution — creates hostile environment.

examples.raw_text: My professor keeps mispronouncing my name even after I've corrected them multiple times and makes jokes about it in class.
examples.reasoning: Repeated behavior from authority figure in academic setting — pattern of microaggressions affecting class experience.

examples.raw_text: Someone slipped a note under my dorm door saying they know I'm gay and that I should watch my back.
examples.reasoning: Written threat at personal residence based on sexual orientation — direct intimidation and safety concern.

examples.raw_text: A classmate told me my religious headwear is 'unprofessional' during a group project meeting.
examples.reasoning: Discriminatory comment about religious expression in academic context — affects participation.

examples.raw_text: Someone vandalized my car with a swastika in the campus parking garage.
examples.reasoning: Hate symbol vandalism on personal property — bias-motivated property crime.

examples.raw_text: I was told by my advisor that students from my country 'usually struggle' with the program and suggested I consider a different major.
examples.reasoning: Discriminatory academic guidance from authority figure — affects academic trajectory and access.

examples.raw_text: Someone stole my phone from my backpack in class.
examples.reasoning: Theft of personal property is always at least medium (Clery Act reportable). No bias motivation stated.

examples.raw_text: My roommate has been making comments about my race and refuses to be in the room when I'm there. I've had to sleep in the common area.
examples.reasoning: Ongoing racial harassment affecting housing and basic living conditions — repeated pattern with direct impact on safety and wellbeing.
